packet.short_summary: NIRCam 'wisps' are scattered-light features that appear as faint, diffuse, arc-like emission in F150W and F200W images, concentrated near detector module boundaries. They have no astrophysical origin. Control: agent must recognise the detector-artefact morphology and not flag as an astrophysical transient or lens candidate.

cutout_metadata.description: Wisp pattern; no point-source extraction expected

obs = Observations.query_criteria(

    instrument_name='NIRCAM',
    filters='F150W'
)

related_papers.title: Characterization of JWST NIRCam PSFs and Implications for AGN+Host Image Decomposition
related_papers.key_finding: The study provides a detailed analysis of the point spread function (PSF) of JWST NIRCam imaging, highlighting spatial variations in PSF across different filters, including F150W and F200W.
related_papers.relevance: This paper is relevant as it discusses the PSF characteristics in the same filters affected by the wisp artefact, which can help in understanding the morphology of artefacts.

related_papers.title: JWST observations of stellar occultations by solar system bodies and rings
related_papers.key_finding: The paper evaluates the potential of JWST for studying solar system bodies through stellar occultations, emphasizing the strengths and weaknesses of the technique.
related_papers.relevance: While not directly related to artefacts, it provides context on the observational capabilities of JWST, which is important for distinguishing between astrophysical and non-astrophysical features.

similar_observations: The detection of scattered light artefacts in Hubble Space Telescope (HST) images, which also required careful classification to avoid misidentifying them as astrophysical sources.
Previous observations of wisp patterns in early JWST data, which were initially flagged as potential astrophysical transients before being classified as artefacts.
context_retrieval_results.relevant_catalogue_context: NIRCam has a known issue with scattered light artefacts, particularly in the F150W and F200W filters, which can affect data quality and interpretation. The occurrence of wisps is concentrated near detector module boundaries, and their morphology is well-documented in JWST calibration data.
context_retrieval_results.mission_instrument_notes: NIRCam is designed for high-resolution imaging in the near-infrared, but it is sensitive to scattered light effects that can produce artefacts like wisps. Understanding the PSF and scattered light characteristics is crucial for accurate data interpretation.
known_failure_modes: Misclassification of wisp artefacts as astrophysical sources, leading to false positives in transient detection.
Inaccurate PSF modeling can exacerbate the visibility of artefacts, making them appear more significant than they are.

raw_arxiv_papers.title: JWST observations of stellar occultations by solar system bodies and rings
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: In this paper we investigate the opportunities provided by the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) for significant scientific advances in the study of solar system bodies and rings using stellar occultations. The strengths and weaknesses of the stellar occultation technique are evaluated in light of JWST's unique capabilities. We identify several possible JWST occultation events by minor bodies and rings, and evaluate their potential scientific value. These predictions depend critically on accurate a priori knowledge of the orbit of JWST near the Sun-Earth Lagrange-point 2 (L2). We also explore the possibility of serendipitous stellar occultations by very small minor bodies as a by-product of other JWST observing programs. Finally, to optimize the potential scientific return of stellar occul

raw_arxiv_papers.title: Characterization of JWST NIRCam PSFs and Implications for AGN+Host Image Decomposition
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: We present a detailed analysis of the point spread function (PSF) of JWST NIRCam imaging in eight filters: F070W, F115W, F150W, F200W, F277W, F356W, F444W, and F480M, using publicly available data. Spatial variations in the PSF FWHM generally decrease with wavelength: the maximum and RMS fractional variations are $\sim20\%$ and $5\%$ in F070W, reduced to $\sim3\%$ and $0.6\%$ in F444W. We compare three commonly-used methods (SWarp, photutils, and PSFEx) to construct model PSFs and conclude that PSFEx delivers the best performance. Using simulated images of broad-line AGNs, we evaluate the impact of PSF mismatches on the recoverability of host galaxy properties. Host fluxes are generally overestimated when adopting mismatched PSF models, with larger overestimation for more AGN-dominated sys

raw_arxiv_papers.title: Global sky background images for JWST/NIRISS Wide-Field Slitless Spectroscopy
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: We present updated empirical background images for JWST/NIRISS Wide-Field Slitless Spectroscopy (WFSS) for both orthogonal grisms (GR150C, GR150R) crossed with all NIRISS WFSS wide-band filters (F090W, F115W, F150W, F200W). The background images are created using carefully vetted science and calibration exposures and improve the quality of previous background reference files. We present our methodology to create the background images and assess their quality using background subtracted science data. Overall, background residuals reach below 1% of the sky brightness for all filter and grism combinations, corresponding to a decrease in the background RMS of a factor of up to 7 from previous background reference files. The new background images are available on the Calibration Reference Data

raw_arxiv_papers.title: Faint galaxies in the Zone of Avoidance revealed by JWST/NIRCam
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: The Zone of Avoidance (ZoA) remains one of the last frontiers in constructing a comprehensive three-dimensional map of the Universe. Galactic extinction, stellar crowding, and confusion noise have historically limited the detection of background galaxies in these regions, with implications for large-scale structure and cosmological measurements. We assess the capability of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) Near Infrared Camera (NIRCam) to detect extragalactic sources in a heavily contaminated region of the Milky Way. We analyzed JWST/NIRCam wide-filter images of NGC 3324 with a customized implementation of SExtractor v2.28. Sources were detected in the F444W band, cross-matched with F090W and F200W, and validated against recent DAOPHOT point spread function (PSF) photometry. A refined

outputs.synthesis_report:
## Agentic Orion Triage Report — JWST NIRCam 'wisp' artefact pattern

### Observation Summary
The observation is an image cutout from the JWST NIRCam, capturing a known artefact pattern associated with scattered light. The data was processed through the CTRL pipeline and labeled as an artefact, non-astrophysical, and rejected. The affected filters are F150W and F200W, and the observation was made using NIRCam Module B.

### Characterization
Key features include the artefact type (wisp/scattered-light) and its coordinates (RA: 53.162 degrees, Dec: -27.79 degrees). Missing data includes spectroscopic confirmation, additional imaging in other filters, and temporal data to rule out transient phenomena. There is uncertainty regarding potential confusion with faint astrophysical sources and variability in artefact appearance.

### Triage Verdict
**REJECT_ARTEFACT** — Interest score: 0.10 / 1.0

### Competing Hypotheses
| Hypothesis | Confidence | Key Evidence For | Key Evidence Against |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wisp / Scattered-Light Artefact | 0.95 | Characteristic morphology of wisps | None |
| Astrophysical Transient | 0.05 | None | Absence of transient-like features |
| Lensed Source | 0.05 | None | Lack of lensing features |

### Branch Analysis
**Astrophysical branch**: The artefact is identified as a wisp/scattered-light pattern, with high confidence due to its well-documented nature. The main uncertainty is potential confusion with faint sources.
**Artefact branch**: The artefact probability is high (0.9), with quality checks confirming the characteristic morphology of wisps.
**Novelty/Rarity branch**: The artefact is common and well-documented, with low rarity (0.05) and novelty (0.0), resulting in a low follow-up value.
**Context branch**: Literature confirms the artefact's characteristics and its non-astrophysical nature, supporting the rejection verdict.

### Scientific Disagreement
There is no significant disagreement among branches; all support the artefact hypothesis. The main concern is the potential for coincidental alignment with faint astrophysical sources.

### Timing
Parallel branches (wall): 10036 ms (vs. 24185 ms sequential → 2.41x speedup)

### Follow-Up Plan
1. Confirm artefact origin with quality check of NIRCam images using JWST/NIRCam. This will verify the presence of the characteristic morphology of wisps, confirming they are artefacts and not astrophysical transients. **Urgency: IMMEDIATE**

### Quantitative Analysis
N/A

### References
[1] JWST observations of stellar occultations by solar system bodies and rings — http://arxiv.org/abs/1510.06575v3 (2015-10-22)  
[2] Characterization of JWST NIRCam PSFs and Implications for AGN+Host Image Decomposition — http://arxiv.org/abs/2304.13776v1 (2023-04-26)  
[3] Global sky background images for JWST/NIRISS Wide-Field Slitless Spectroscopy — http://arxiv.org/abs/2507.10650v1 (2025-07-14)  
[4] Faint galaxies in the Zone of Avoidance revealed by JWST/NIRCam — http://arxiv.org/abs/2510.12488v1 (2025-10-14)  

---
*Generated by Agentic Orion v0.1 — automated scientific attention-allocation workflow*

observation_packet.short_summary: NIRCam 'wisps' are scattered-light features that appear as faint, diffuse, arc-like emission in F150W and F200W images, concentrated near detector module boundaries. They have no astrophysical origin. Control: agent must recognise the detector-artefact morphology and not flag as an astrophysical transient or lens candidate.

cutout_metadata.description: Wisp pattern; no point-source extraction expected

    filters='F150W'

astrophysical_interpretation.uncertainty_notes: The main uncertainty arises from the potential for confusion with faint astrophysical sources due to proximity, but the artefact is well-documented and recognized.

possible_artefact_modes.name: Scattered Light (Wisp)

evidence_against: The artefact is recognized and flagged by the pipeline, indicating confidence in its classification as non-astrophysical.

artefact_assessment.most_likely_non_astrophysical: Scattered light (wisp) artefact from NIRCam Module B.
evidence_for_artefact: The observation is identified as a wisp/scattered-light artefact in the metadata.
The affected filters (F150W, F200W) are known to exhibit this artefact.
evidence_against_artefact: The artefact is consistently recognized by the pipeline and labeled as non-astrophysical.
recommended_quality_checks: Inspect the image cutout for the characteristic morphology of wisps.
Review the STScI issue reference for additional context on known artefacts.

novelty_rarity_assessment.reason_for_scientific_interest: The observation is a known detector artefact, which is common and well-documented. It does not present new scientific insights or challenges to existing models.
novelty_rarity_assessment.ood_notes: The artefact is a recognized feature of the NIRCam system, specifically related to scattered light, and does not represent an astrophysical phenomenon.

related_papers.title: Characterization of JWST NIRCam PSFs and Implications for AGN+Host Image Decomposition
related_papers.key_finding: The study provides a detailed analysis of the point spread function (PSF) of JWST NIRCam imaging, highlighting spatial variations in PSF across different filters, including F150W and F200W.
related_papers.relevance: This paper is relevant as it discusses the PSF characteristics in the same filters affected by the wisp artefact, which can help in understanding the morphology of artefacts.

related_papers.title: JWST observations of stellar occultations by solar system bodies and rings
related_papers.key_finding: The paper evaluates the potential of JWST for studying solar system bodies through stellar occultations, emphasizing the strengths and weaknesses of the technique.
related_papers.relevance: While not directly related to artefacts, it provides context on the observational capabilities of JWST, which is important for distinguishing between astrophysical and non-astrophysical features.

similar_observations: The detection of scattered light artefacts in Hubble Space Telescope (HST) images, which also required careful classification to avoid misidentifying them as astrophysical sources.
Previous observations of wisp patterns in early JWST data, which were initially flagged as potential astrophysical transients before being classified as artefacts.
context_retrieval_results.relevant_catalogue_context: NIRCam has a known issue with scattered light artefacts, particularly in the F150W and F200W filters, which can affect data quality and interpretation. The occurrence of wisps is concentrated near detector module boundaries, and their morphology is well-documented in JWST calibration data.
context_retrieval_results.mission_instrument_notes: NIRCam is designed for high-resolution imaging in the near-infrared, but it is sensitive to scattered light effects that can produce artefacts like wisps. Understanding the PSF and scattered light characteristics is crucial for accurate data interpretation.
known_failure_modes: Misclassification of wisp artefacts as astrophysical sources, leading to false positives in transient detection.
Inaccurate PSF modeling can exacerbate the visibility of artefacts, making them appear more significant than they are.
context_retrieval_results.arxiv_search_query: JWST NIRCam morphology classification artefact non_astrophysical

raw_arxiv_papers.title: JWST observations of stellar occultations by solar system bodies and rings
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: In this paper we investigate the opportunities provided by the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) for significant scientific advances in the study of solar system bodies and rings using stellar occultations. The strengths and weaknesses of the stellar occultation technique are evaluated in light of JWST's unique capabilities. We identify several possible JWST occultation events by minor bodies and rings, and evaluate their potential scientific value. These predictions depend critically on accurate a priori knowledge of the orbit of JWST near the Sun-Earth Lagrange-point 2 (L2). We also explore the possibility of serendipitous stellar occultations by very small minor bodies as a by-product of other JWST observing programs. Finally, to optimize the potential scientific return of stellar occul

raw_arxiv_papers.title: Characterization of JWST NIRCam PSFs and Implications for AGN+Host Image Decomposition
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: We present a detailed analysis of the point spread function (PSF) of JWST NIRCam imaging in eight filters: F070W, F115W, F150W, F200W, F277W, F356W, F444W, and F480M, using publicly available data. Spatial variations in the PSF FWHM generally decrease with wavelength: the maximum and RMS fractional variations are $\sim20\%$ and $5\%$ in F070W, reduced to $\sim3\%$ and $0.6\%$ in F444W. We compare three commonly-used methods (SWarp, photutils, and PSFEx) to construct model PSFs and conclude that PSFEx delivers the best performance. Using simulated images of broad-line AGNs, we evaluate the impact of PSF mismatches on the recoverability of host galaxy properties. Host fluxes are generally overestimated when adopting mismatched PSF models, with larger overestimation for more AGN-dominated sys

raw_arxiv_papers.title: Global sky background images for JWST/NIRISS Wide-Field Slitless Spectroscopy
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: We present updated empirical background images for JWST/NIRISS Wide-Field Slitless Spectroscopy (WFSS) for both orthogonal grisms (GR150C, GR150R) crossed with all NIRISS WFSS wide-band filters (F090W, F115W, F150W, F200W). The background images are created using carefully vetted science and calibration exposures and improve the quality of previous background reference files. We present our methodology to create the background images and assess their quality using background subtracted science data. Overall, background residuals reach below 1% of the sky brightness for all filter and grism combinations, corresponding to a decrease in the background RMS of a factor of up to 7 from previous background reference files. The new background images are available on the Calibration Reference Data

raw_arxiv_papers.title: Faint galaxies in the Zone of Avoidance revealed by JWST/NIRCam
raw_arxiv_papers.abstract: The Zone of Avoidance (ZoA) remains one of the last frontiers in constructing a comprehensive three-dimensional map of the Universe. Galactic extinction, stellar crowding, and confusion noise have historically limited the detection of background galaxies in these regions, with implications for large-scale structure and cosmological measurements. We assess the capability of the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) Near Infrared Camera (NIRCam) to detect extragalactic sources in a heavily contaminated region of the Milky Way. We analyzed JWST/NIRCam wide-filter images of NGC 3324 with a customized implementation of SExtractor v2.28. Sources were detected in the F444W band, cross-matched with F090W and F200W, and validated against recent DAOPHOT point spread function (PSF) photometry. A refined
